statement.markdown: Implement a Queue data structure using a doubly linked list. The queue should support enqueue and dequeue operations. A dequeue operation can remove an element from either the front or the rear of the queue. The queue should also have a method to check if it's empty.
statement.inputFormat: Input is provided through the queue methods. The queue will be empty initially.
statement.outputFormat: The queue operations should return the result according to the problem statement.
statement.notes: The queue's enqueue and dequeue operations should be O(1) on average.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the queue

hints.text: Pay attention to the enqueue and dequeue operations, as well as the checkEmpty method.
Consider the edge cases, such as an empty queue or a queue with a single element.

editorial.title: Queue Implementation
editorial.intuition: To implement the queue, we can use a doubly linked list to store the elements.
editorial.bruteForce: We can implement the queue by iterating through the linked list for each operation.
editorial.betterSolution: By maintaining a pointer to the rear and front of the queue, we can achieve O(1) average time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a doubly linked list and maintaining pointers to the front and rear of the queue.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The queue operations work as follows: enqueue adds an element to the rear, dequeue removes an element from the front, and checkEmpty returns true if the queue is empty.
editorial.dryRun: To understand the optimal solution, consider the following example: enqueue(1), enqueue(2), dequeue(), dequeue().
commonMistakes: Failing to update the rear pointer after enqueueing an element
Failing to update the front pointer after dequeuing an element
